premise: At a privately run residential hotel for the old and broke, a cleaner who grew up in the building starts following the nightly advice of the foulmouthed saint residents swear rides the service elevator. When a new manager begins clearing floors by rule and arrears, she uses maintenance rounds, meal lines, and petty theft to keep her little city from being broken apart.
engine_guess: contained institutional siege with sly miracles
protagonist_descriptor: hotel cleaner raised among permanent tenants
pressure_descriptor: privatized eviction logic versus improvised care
setting_descriptor: failing single-room occupancy hotel in a modern city
why_it_might_work: The premise puts tenderness inside a pressure-cooker institution and gives the protagonist concrete daily labor she can exploit. The saint may be real or may be the building's shared invention, which lets the book hold mystery without surrendering social detail.
risk: The mythic element could turn merely whimsical if the hotel's economics stay vague. The caretaking plot also needs enough moral dirt and comedy to avoid feeling pious.

premise: After a heat wave kills hundreds when the city's new emergency-alert system routes pleas for help into dead queues, a circle of retired switchboard operators becomes obsessed with reconstructing the last unanswered calls. Their private dossier drags them from condolence visits to public hearings to server rooms as officials race to declare the failure solved before the next storm.
protagonist_descriptor: retired phone operators with unfinished civic loyalty
pressure_descriptor: mass death, incoming weather, and a fixable lie
setting_descriptor: near-future American city under climate-tech governance
why_it_might_work: It offers documentary texture, late-life protagonists, and a public disaster that stays intimate because every clue begins as a voice. The operators' obsession can generate both procedural momentum and messy personal reawakenings.
risk: The inquiry could become too report-like if the mourners and operators do not remain vivid as people. It also needs to avoid treating the technology failure as a simple villain.

premise: In an archipelago where shrine-radios tell each island how to fish, pray, and vote, the women who crawl inside the transmitters to scrape out salt and bone keep a secret handbook of glitches, omens, and lies. Over fifteen years of storms, strikes, and disappearances, their notes become the only record that the voices have begun slipping private memories into the national script.
engine_guess: collective secret history through dangerous maintenance work
protagonist_descriptor: rotating crew of transmitter cleaners and repairers
pressure_descriptor: shared secrecy under a voice-system that rewrites reality
setting_descriptor: industrial island chain ruled by sacred broadcast infrastructure
why_it_might_work: The labor is physical and peculiar, the plurality is built in, and the secret handbook gives the novel a spine without trapping it in bureaucracy. The premise can move between satire, dread, apprenticeship, travel, and intimate self-doubt as the women age into and out of the work.
risk: It will fail if the worldbuilding overwhelms character interiority or if the radios' metaphysics stay mushy. The long span also needs sharp selection to avoid reading like a chronicle.

premise: In a river port where the dead must be washed, perfumed, and dressed before sunrise or they begin dreaming through the living, a family of body-washers takes lovers among mourners, smugglers, and rival undertakers while preparing the city for its most crowded holy season. When a celebrated courtesan's corpse starts waking in different bodies on different nights, their intimate trade becomes a public fight over desire, sanctity, and ownership of the dead.
engine_guess: sensual mortuary procedural with public haunting
pressure_descriptor: holy-season deadlines and a beloved body that will not stay put
setting_descriptor: historical river city built around funerary ritual
why_it_might_work: The book would have a strong bodily engine, a collective protagonist, and procedures that naturally produce intimacy, gossip, rivalry, and spiritual consequence. The haunting is legible enough to drive plot while leaving room for tenderness and erotic mess.
risk: The sensuality and supernatural premise could tip into lush sameness if the civic stakes stay abstract. It also needs discipline not to sentimentalize the dead or flatten the living into archetypes.

premise: In the stacked city of Bellglass, poor families rent mechanical angels to keep their apartments anchored in time, and a fourteen-year-old repair apprentice inherits both her mother's debt and her mother's failing angel after an accident. When the machine begins answering in her mother's voice and demanding impossible repairs, she is pulled into the city's underside, her first fierce attachments, and the question of whether saving her block means feeding more children into the system that is devouring it.
engine_guess: coming-of-age repair quest inside a debt-strapped uncanny city
protagonist_descriptor: adolescent angel mechanic born into tenement debt
pressure_descriptor: inheritance debt, first desire, and a neighborhood's survival
setting_descriptor: vertical city held together by rented clockwork angels
why_it_might_work: The premise marries intimate adolescence to a concrete class machine, so every repair has emotional and social cost. The angel's voice lets tenderness and grief stay active inside a plot that can widen into neighborhood consequence.
risk: It could slide toward familiar YA beats if the city feels generic or the debt system too allegorical. The tragic register also needs enough surprise to avoid solemn monotony.

premise: At a private weather-defense campus outside a drought city, the compliance director learns that the cloud-seeding model everyone calls objective was tuned to match an old rainmaking liturgy hidden inside the code. As rationing, drills, and neighborhood panic spread over a brutal year, she starts an affair with the forecaster selling salvation on television and discovers which districts the company has already decided not to save.
engine_guess: erotic institutional thriller under climate panic
protagonist_descriptor: compliance director with a talent for self-deception
pressure_descriptor: escalating drought, corporate secrecy, and compromised desire
setting_descriptor: semi-enclosed weather-tech campus above a thirsty metropolis
why_it_might_work: The premise joins bodily appetite, professional procedure, and a high-stakes external crisis without requiring literal magic. Mythic pressure enters as belief and rhetoric, which can sharpen satire while keeping the reality principle hard.
risk: If the drought remains background atmosphere, the novel will feel airless and overdesigned. The affair and conspiracy also need psychological specificity to avoid stock-thriller motion.
